docs.title: LT Consultant Competencies
docs.text:
The competency framework for Language Technology Consultants. 42 competencies across 5 categories, each with its rationale, target outcome, and a progression of suggested activities.

Use the navigation (or search) to browse by category. Every page here is generated from the hand-authored source in competencies/ — edit a descriptor there to change what appears on the site.

Category Competencies Core Technical 5 Technology Domain 18 Core 10 Professional 4 Education 5

See the Coverage page for which competencies have training modules and which are still gaps.

docs.title: Competency Coverage
docs.text:
Last generated: 2026-07-22.

21 of 43 competencies have at least one module. 22 are gaps (no module yet).

docs.title: Adult Education
docs.text: Category: Core

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
Applies methods suited to training adults in diverse contexts.

Observable criteria

  • Identifies the specific needs of adult learners (technical level, culture, etc.).
  • Uses participatory, hands-on approaches to encourage active learning.
  • Adapts teaching style to cultural differences.

Why it matters

  • Training adults involves varying methods (demonstrations, hands-on exercises, discussions) and respecting each person’s learning pace.

docs.title: Theory
docs.text:
Knows the main principles of adult education and can explain them simply.

Observable criteria

  • Explains the basics of active learning (dialogue, role-plays, hands-on activities, etc.).
  • Recognizes that learners have diverse styles (auditory, visual, kinesthetic).

Why it matters

  • A solid grasp of adult education theory enhances session design and delivery, leading to better outcomes and knowledge transfer.

docs.title: Consulting Process Skills
docs.text: Category: Core · Guide section: Skills Required for a Consulting Session

docs.title: Participatory Approaches
docs.text:
Uses methods that encourage active participation from learners or the teams being supported.

Observable criteria

  • Arranges a discovery session to identify needs and issues.
  • Proposes ideas or solutions in a coaching-style approach.
  • Engages participants in evaluation and decision-making.

Why it matters

  • Participatory methods promote user empowerment and ensure that proposed solutions are well accepted by the teams.

docs.title: Implementation and Evaluation
docs.text:
Guides the implementation of solutions and helps evaluate their effectiveness with the team.

Observable criteria

  • Monitors progress after training or an intervention.
  • Checks whether results meet expectations and discusses them with the team.
  • Communicates successes and areas for improvement.

Why it matters

  • Assisting teams in applying and assessing solutions ensures genuine adoption and provides a foundation for future adjustments.

docs.title: Establishing a Procedure
docs.text:
Sets up a clear framework for the consulting or support process.

Observable criteria

  • Defines objectives, roles, and responsibilities at the outset.
  • Listens to the organization’s or team’s needs.
  • Outlines the steps in the consultation process, from initial diagnosis to final evaluation.

Why it matters

  • A shared, well-defined procedure makes sure everyone understands the phases and expected outcomes.

docs.title: Interpersonal Skills
docs.text: Category: Core · Guide section: Interpersonal Competencies

docs.title: Responsibility
docs.text:
Effectively manages commitments and keeps one’s word.

Observable criteria

  • Meets deadlines and objectives.
  • Adjusts workload to avoid burnout.
  • Is accountable to colleagues and supervisors.

Why it matters

  • Being reliable and capable of managing responsibilities well builds trust and enhances service quality.

docs.title: Self-Awareness
docs.text:
Understands personal strengths and weaknesses and takes steps to improve when working in a team.

Observable criteria

  • Recognizes how one’s personality affects collaborative work.
  • Accepts feedback without getting upset unnecessarily.
  • Seeks to address personal weaknesses in a constructive way.

Why it matters

  • Greater self-awareness leads to better interactions and adaptability in various work contexts.

docs.title: Social Skills
docs.text:
Communicates respectfully and constructively, and maintains good relationships within a team.

Observable criteria

  • Actively listens to others.
  • Gives and receives criticism in a constructive manner.
  • Offers guidance and encourages personal development.
  • Recognizes achievements and addresses conflicts with tact.

Why it matters

  • Positive professional relationships are crucial for creating an environment that fosters collaboration and successful projects.

docs.title: Language Use
docs.text: Category: Core · Guide section: Language and Culture

docs.title: Social Anthropology
docs.text:
Respects cultural differences and knows how to work according to local norms.

Observable criteria

  • Observes and learns about the customs and practices of the community.
  • Interacts with empathy and respect for cultural differences.
  • Adapts communication style to local cultural standards.

Why it matters

  • A sound cultural understanding helps ensure that communities accept the consultant’s work and remain engaged with the tools and processes.

docs.title: Use of Language
docs.text:
Communicates clearly in the most appropriate vehicular language.

Observable criteria

  • Expresses ideas clearly and understandably, both verbally and in writing.
  • Chooses the right language for the context to ensure comprehension by all.
  • Simplifies technical concepts when explaining to non-specialists.

Why it matters

  • Adequate proficiency in the local or vehicular language is crucial for training and assisting teams without communication barriers.

docs.title: Mentoring
docs.text: Category: Core

docs.title: Attitudes
docs.text:
Creates a positive environment that encourages mentees to grow professionally.

Observable criteria

  • Shows genuine care, celebrates successes, and recognizes personal limits.
  • Believes in the mentee’s potential and highlights their achievements.

Why it matters

  • Attitudes of listening and encouragement are key to establishing trust and motivating mentees.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
Understands the difference between formal mentoring, informal mentoring, coaching, and facilitation.

Observable criteria

  • Explains how formal mentoring works (objectives, roles, duration).
  • Clarifies mutual expectations (mentor, mentee, supervisor) at the start.

Why it matters

  • A clear framework (objectives, boundaries, follow-up) ensures mentoring is effective and that each person knows their role.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Uses the necessary know-how to guide and support mentees.

Observable criteria

  • Sets specific objectives with the mentee and follows up regularly.
  • Provides respectful and constructive feedback.
  • Maintains confidentiality regarding shared experiences.

Why it matters

  • Mentoring strengthens everyone’s ability to learn, to train others, and to contribute to the overall development of the team.

docs.title: Professional Networking
docs.text: Category: Core · Guide section: Relationships with Other Organizations

docs.title: Influencing and Building a Network
docs.text:
Maintains respectful and constructive relationships with external partners, either to learn from them or to have a positive influence.

Observable criteria

  • Clearly communicates the project’s values and needs in the LT field.
  • Represents local linguistic communities by highlighting their challenges and priorities.
  • Seeks potential partners or useful contacts.

Why it matters

  • Developing a network and advocating for project needs help secure support and foster productive collaborations.

docs.title: Relationship with Partners
docs.text:
Collaborates effectively with a range of partners and represents one’s own organization professionally.

Observable criteria

  • Explains the importance of language technology to non-specialists.
  • Respects the principles and values of other organizations.
  • Aims for mutually beneficial (win-win) solutions.

Why it matters

  • Positive partnerships can lead to new funding opportunities, technical support, and greater recognition of the work being done.

docs.title: Program Design and Engagement
docs.text: Category: Core · Guide section: Program Design and Mobilization

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Considers cultural, social, and economic factors when designing or implementing a language/technology program or project.

Observable criteria

  • Analyzes local factors that may affect adoption of new technology tools.
  • Engages the community to ensure ownership of the project.

Why it matters

  • Designing a program that reflects local socio-linguistic realities increases its relevance and sustainability.

docs.title: Involvement
docs.text:
Works respectfully and responsibly with the community and stakeholders.

Observable criteria

  • Recognizes the community’s rights and ownership of its own project.
  • Participates in administrative details (intellectual property rights, licenses, etc.) as needed.
  • Maintains a positive attitude toward partners, even when disagreements arise.

Why it matters

  • A consultant’s involvement throughout all phases of a project, while respecting each partner’s role, strengthens credibility and effectiveness.

docs.title: Strategy
docs.text:
Understands the basic principles for developing and managing a language or technology project.

Observable criteria

  • Identifies key stages of a program (planning, implementation, evaluation).
  • Contributes constructive ideas for continuous program improvement.
  • Explains why it is important to involve a variety of disciplines.

Why it matters

  • Knowing the strategic aspects of a program helps consultants better assist teams and coordinate available resources.

docs.title: Scholarship and Documentation
docs.text: Category: Core · Guide section: Research and Documentation

docs.title: Intellectual Property
docs.text:
Understands basic concepts of copyright and licensing for resources and technology tools.

Observable criteria

  • Distinguishes between different license types (copyright, Creative Commons, etc.).
  • Adheres to copyright regulations in all projects.

Why it matters

  • Working ethically and legally in terms of intellectual property rights protects the project and encourages responsible resource sharing.

docs.title: Publication and Archiving
docs.text:
Knows how to document and archive resources so they remain accessible and reusable.

Observable criteria

  • Writes training or reference materials that are clear and concise.
  • Classifies and archives documents (text files, databases, etc.) in an organized manner.

Why it matters

  • Good document management capitalizes on completed work and avoids duplication of effort in future projects.

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
Keeps abreast of new developments and contributes, at one’s own level, to improving practices or research in the field.

Observable criteria

  • Stays informed about updates (software, methods, etc.).
  • Shares experiences or observations with the community.
  • Asks questions and actively participates in trainings, workshops, or conferences.

Why it matters

  • Staying current and exchanging best practices fosters innovation and increases the quality of services provided.

docs.title: Technology for Consulting
docs.text: Category: Core · Guide section: Technologies Used for Consulting

docs.title: Communication and Collaboration Tools
docs.text:
Effectively uses digital tools for remote training, collaboration, and file-sharing.

Observable criteria

  • Is proficient with videoconferencing, email, and instant messaging tools.
  • Securely shares documents (cloud, version tracking) with team members.
  • Clarifies expectations and roles at the outset of any online consulting.

Why it matters

  • Making good use of communication tools saves time and improves the quality of collaborative work, both in person and remotely.

docs.title: Data Organization
docs.text:
Manages, backs up, and protects digital data responsibly.

Observable criteria

  • Maintains computers and keeps software updated.
  • Regularly backs up and organizes files so they are easy to find.
  • Protects data confidentiality (passwords, limited access, etc.).

Why it matters

  • Proper data management and backups ensure continuity of work and protect sensitive information.

docs.title: Working in a Multicultural Environment
docs.text: Category: Core

docs.title: Intercultural Communication
docs.text:
Communicates with people from other cultures in a respectful and appropriate manner.

Observable criteria

  • Identifies different communication styles and adapts accordingly.
  • Recognizes that leadership or conflict management can vary by culture.
  • Remains flexible and open-minded in intercultural training and mentoring.

Why it matters

  • Good intercultural communication fosters collaboration and prevents misunderstandings that could hinder teamwork.

docs.title: Multicultural Work
docs.text:
Works effectively in teams or projects involving people of diverse cultural backgrounds.

Observable criteria

  • Considers cultural differences when encouraging positive group dynamics.
  • Actively participates in decision-making by including all voices.
  • Respects local authorities and customs.

Why it matters

  • Understanding and valuing cultural differences contribute to a harmonious work environment and better project outcomes.
  • Technical problem-solving
  • Interpersonal and intercultural competencies
  • Adult education and mentoring
  • Data organization, communication, and management

docs.title: Computer Hardware
docs.text: Category: Core Technical

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: While hardware support is not a goal of a Language Technologist, in the absence of a dedicated technician, it is often necessary to resolve basic hardware problems before continuing with LT work. This competency invites the candidate to stay up-to-date with the use and troubleshooting of current and coming hardware and its basic usage. Those hoping to specialize in Language Technology will need to strike a healthy balance between LT and IT to progress in LT.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to troubleshoot basic computer hardware problems.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Can effectively use and troubleshoot current and recent computer and mobile hardware
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify the various hardware components of a computer and understand their function. Advanced Beginner Spend time with an IT professional while they troubleshoot computer hardware issues. Practitioner Troubleshoot hardware issues, and recommend appropriate hardware for language program use. Trainer Research the latest hardware components

docs.title: Fonts & Encoding
docs.text: Category: Core Technical

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Some scripts and fonts reinforce cultural or community identity. A Language Technologist is expected to be aware of or investigate the fonts and encodings that can be used for various languages.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able troubleshoot font and character display issues, and convert between encodings.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Can troubleshoot font issues.
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify local scripts and fonts. Advanced Beginner Can identify issues related to fonts, and suggest appropriate solutions. Practitioner Train others to use appropriate fonts. Trainer Research alternate scripts. Learn to create and tweak fonts.

docs.title: 2.0 — Can identify and convert between common document formats.
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Pay attention what file formats are in use in your context (Word, LibreOffice, Publisher, Web Pages, etc.). Advanced Beginner Learn which programs can open and edit various document formats and identify which formats and content can be easily converted to others. Practitioner — Trainer —

docs.title: 3.0 — Can troubleshoot encoding issues
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify common encodings (Legacy/Unicode/NFC/NFD) Advanced Beginner Convert data between common scripts and encodings. Learn about SIL's Open Font License. Practitioner Create and and use a custom encoding conversion, set up a transliteration project in Paratext, etc. Trainer Research encoding in alternate scripts

docs.title: Keyboards
docs.text: Category: Core Technical

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: A large part of interaction with our devices happens through a keyboard. A Language Technologist is expected to help users choose keyboards that are appropriate for language, encoding, and physical layout.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to teach others how to install and configure keyboard solutions for typing language characters.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Configure Virtual Keyboard Solutions
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Install appropriate virtual keyboarding solutions on user computers. Advanced Beginner Configure applications to work with the appropriate keyboards, through system language association and switching Practitioner Work with individuals to install and configure custom keyboarding solutions. Trainer Teach a group how to configure custom keyboard solutions.

docs.title: Malware
docs.text: Category: Core Technical

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Malware issues can stop the progress of a language project. A language technologist needs to be able resolve these issues.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to prevent, identify and resolve common malware issues

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Can prevent, identify and resolve common malware issues
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Scan for and identify malware issues on your devices. Advanced Beginner Learn how to scan for and repair virus infections Practitioner Train users in best practices for choosing and maintaining security software and avoiding malware. Trainer Learn advanced features such as how to use whitelists to protect critical tools from overzealous security software.

docs.title: OS Basics
docs.text: Category: Core Technical

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: It is often necessary to resolve basic operating system configuration problems before continuing with LT work, as well as to keep your machine in working order.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to troubleshoot operating system installation issues, configure for optimal use, and teach best practices.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Can successfully use and troubleshoot current and recent operating systems
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn to identify the operating system and configure basic options. Advanced Beginner Spend time with an IT professional Installing, activating and configuring an operating system. Practitioner Explore and use the latest appropriate operating system, then help users adjust when they upgrade. Trainer Research and test alternative Operating Systems, like Linux, that may serve some users.

docs.title: 2.0 — Configuration for Success
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify user needs, such as language, display, accessibility, and preferences Advanced Beginner Learn to configure OS language and display options on your own devices. Practitioner Assist users to configure their devices for display and preferences. Trainer Research assistive technologies for those with disabilities.

docs.title: General
docs.text:
Category: Education · Workbook label: Domain-specific education

This applies the workbook's single Domain-specific education ladder to the General domain; the framework calls out each domain separately, so the rationale and activities below are shared across them.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Have a good understanding of one of the language domains used for Bible translation and language development work.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Has pursue domain specific knowledge via formal education or equivalent in one of the following: translation, linguistics, literacy / education or scripture engagment.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — -
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Complete a semester of university level courses in one of these domains. Alternately, gain significant practical experience in one of these domains from working in a language program. Advanced Beginner Complete a bachelor's program or a semester of post graduate level courses in one of these domains. Alternately, gain significant practical experience in one of these domains from working in a language program. Practitioner Complete a Master's program in one of these domains or demonstrate a thorough understanding and respected contribution to the field. Trainer Complete a Doctoral program (PhD, EdD) in one of these domains.

docs.title: Community of Practice Involvement
docs.text: Category: Professional · Workbook label: CoP Involvement

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Learning from and sharing with peers helps the language technologist to grow and to serve the user

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Can communicate with peers both to find and to share solutions to problems.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Join an LT Community of Practice (CoP) and learn from the discussions. Advanced Beginner Contribute to a language technology community of practice. Practitioner Contribute regularly to a CoP, offering solutions to problems and fielding questions Trainer Manage an LT CoP

docs.title: English Language
docs.text: Category: Professional

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Many of the LT tools, training materials and documentation are in English only.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Can understand English training and technical publications and convey to others what they learned.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Level A2 in reading on Common European Framework of Reference for Languages Advanced Beginner Level B1 in reading on Common European Framework of Reference for Languages Practitioner Level B2 in reading on Common European Framework of Reference for Languages Trainer Level C1 in reading on Common European Framework of Reference for Languages

docs.title: Problem Solving
docs.text: Category: Professional

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Problem solving is an important skill for dealing with language technology issues and problems

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Demonstrates use of creative, effective, and efficient solutions to complex problems

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Find and complete problem-solving puzzles. Advanced Beginner Diagnose common problems by identifying the individual components of the problem and seek assistance. Practitioner Find and share effective workarounds for commonly experienced problems. Trainer Develop and document complete solutions and processes to solve complex problems.

docs.title: Responsive Feedback
docs.text: Category: Professional

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Giving feedback to the developers of language technology tools is an important skill for improving these tools.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Has the ability to describe a software issue in a meaningful way for developers to act on.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn what should be included in a bug report. Advanced Beginner Submit a well defined bug report or a new feature request for developers to act upon. Follow the report or request through the process. Practitioner Actively participate as a beta tester for a language technology tool. Trainer Represents the user community on software development prioritization committees, UX design, or in championing new products.

docs.title: Archiving and Copyright Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain · Workbook label: Archiving & Copyright

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Perservation of our data is important. It prevents data loss, meets legal requirements and increases secuity.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Can promote archiving and proper use and licensing of intellectual property.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify the various Creative Commons licenses. Learn what metadata is. Complete the SIL intellectual property courses. Advanced Beginner Learn how to use the licensing components properly in various LT tools. Practitioner Learn how to use REAP and RAMP for archiving data Trainer/Proficient Teach others to use the licensing components in LT tools Expert Teach others to use REAP and RAMP

docs.title: Artificial Intelligence (AI) Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: AI tools have become foundational to modern language technology work. This competency enables consultants to amplify their productivity, offload routine cognitive work, sharpen decision-making, and accelerate innovation. Just as importantly, it equips consultants to evaluate AI output critically and to apply AI responsibly in contexts where outputs touch Scripture, sensitive minority-language data, and the trust of partner communities.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to apply general and specialized AI tools effectively, critically, and responsibly in language technology work, and can guide others in their use.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General AI Tools (LLMs and AI Assistants)
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Can get useful results from a general AI assistant (e.g., Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Copilot) for everyday tasks such as drafting, summarizing, brainstorming, and explaining concepts. Understands that AI can hallucinate and that outputs require verification. Advanced Beginner Crafts effective prompts using techniques such as clear context-setting, role assignment, few-shot examples, and structured-output requests. Can compare and choose among major tools for different needs. Practitioner Collaborates with AI assistants as part of regular workflow — using multi-turn refinement, projects/custom instructions, file and image uploads, document analysis, and built-in connectors (Drive, email, calendars, etc.). Knows when AI is not the right tool. Trainer/Proficient Trains and coaches others in effective use of general AI tools, including running workshops, producing reference material, and providing one-on-one support. Expert —

docs.title: 2.0 — Specialized Language and Translation AI Tools
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Can explain which categories of specialized AI tools apply to which language-work tasks — e.g., neural machine translation, automated quality assessment (AQuA), Greek/Hebrew study tools (Greek Room), automatic speech recognition for low-resource languages (MMS, Whisper), text-to-speech, OCR for non-Latin scripts, alignment, and back-translation tools. Advanced Beginner Uses available specialized AI tools, including SIL-internal tools, for concrete tasks with guidance. Practitioner Uses available specialized AI tools, including SIL-internal tools, for concrete tasks with guidance. Trainer/Proficient Trains and supports users across language teams and domains in adopting specialized AI tools. Provides structured feedback to tool developers and shares best practices across the consultant community Expert —

docs.title: 3.0 — Critical Evaluation and Quality Assurance of AI Output
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Recognizes that AI outputs can be wrong, biased, or fabricated — and that this risk is heightened for low-resource languages and for theological, cultural, and named-entity content. Advanced Beginner Routinely verifies AI-generated content against authoritative sources. Identifies common failure modes (hallucinated citations, mistranslated proper nouns, dropped negations, register drift, doctrinal inaccuracy, fluent-but-wrong output) Practitioner Designs and applies systematic review processes for AI-assisted work — spot-checking, back-translation comparison, reference-matching, and human-in-the-loop validation appropriate to the stakes of the task. Trainer/Proficient Trains others in evaluation methods and helps establish quality-assurance standards for AI use within their team or project. Expert —

docs.title: 4.0 — Ethical, Responsible, and Secure Use of AI
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Understands that data submitted to AI tools may be stored, used for model training, or exposed — and that unpublished translations, partner data, and community language data require special care. Aware of relevant SIL and Wycliffe AI guidelines. Advanced Beginner Applies basic data-handling practices: redacting sensitive content, choosing tools with appropriate privacy/retention settings, respecting community ownership of language data, and disclosing AI-assisted work appropriately. Practitioner Evaluates AI tools for fitness in a given context — weighing privacy, licensing, cost, cultural appropriateness, and theological reliability — and advises colleagues on tool selection. Trainer/Proficient Trains and advises others on responsible AI practice, and helps shape local guidelines for AI use with the language communities they serve. Expert —

docs.title: 5.0 — AI Workflow Integration and Automation
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Understands that AI can be embedded into larger workflows beyond chat interfaces — through APIs, agents, scripts, IDE integrations, custom assistants (Projects, Custom GPTs, Gems), and connectors to tools like Drive, email, and code repositories. Advanced Beginner Uses pre-built AI integrations to streamline routine work — for example, AI features inside Office, VS Code, or browsers; configured Projects/Custom GPTs with system prompts and reference files; AI-assisted search across personal documents. Practitioner Builds simple AI-augmented automations or lightweight agents to handle recurring language-tech tasks — for example, using Claude Code or Copilot to generate and run scripts, chaining steps with tools like n8n or Zapier, or producing small custom assistants tuned to a specific consulting workflow. Trainer/Proficient Designs and shares reusable AI-augmented workflows, scripts, or agents with the wider consultant community. Mentors others in safely extending their own work with automation. Expert —

docs.title: Computational and Corpus Linguistics
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: This competency promotes the use of natural language processing tools for the advancement of the development of language ands its community.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to use and teach computational tools for corpus and data analysis

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Corpus Linguistics
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Explore existing tools for computational and corpus linguistics. Advanced Beginner Learn to collect, prepare and tag corpus data for Natural Language Processing. Practitioner Learn to use an NLP tool for forced alignment, semantic parsing, or corpus linguistics. Trainer/Proficient Train users to use an NLP tool for forced alignment, semantic parsing, or corpus linguistics. Expert Participate or contribute to augment, improve, or develop new NLP methods.

docs.title: 2.0 — Machine Learning
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Explore existing tools for machine learning. Advanced Beginner Try out some machine learning tools. Practitioner Contribute to a project using existing machine learning to explore solutions. Trainer/Proficient Train users to use existing machine learning solutions. Expert Participate or contribute to augment, improve, or develop new Machine Learning projects.

docs.title: Data Conversion
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Many time language Technologists need to assist a language workers to cleanup their data to make it consistent or to convert their data from one format to another.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Can use and teach a scripting language to do data conversion and cleanup.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn how to do advanced search and replace in text Advanced Beginner Learn to use a scripting language to replace strings according to context Practitioner Use a scripting language environment to clean up or convert data. Trainer/Proficient Teach and mentor others in the use of a scripting language environment to clean up or convert data. Expert Use a programming language (i.e. Perl or Python) to write more complex data conversion or cleanup routines.

docs.title: Digital and Print Publishing
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: This competency covers the usage and training of users that help them to publish work in print form and digitially.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to use the tools that assist language workers to publish their work electronically and on paper and is able to consult others on their use.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Print Publishing
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify appropriate tools to produce draft print output of Scripture and dictionaries Advanced Beginner Use appropriate tools to produce draft print output of Scripture and dictionaries Practitioner Customize and use appropriate tools to produce publishable output for Scripture and dictionaries Trainer/Proficient Teach and mentor others to customize and use appropriate tools to produce publishable output for Scripture and dictionaries Expert Research the latest developments in current publishing tools

docs.title: 2.0 — Digital Publishing
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify appropriate tools to produce digital output of Scripture and dictionaries for web and mobile. Advanced Beginner Use appropriate tools to produce digital output of Scripture and dictionaries for web and mobile. Practitioner Customize and use appropriate tools to produce publishable output for Scripture and dictionaries Trainer/Proficient Teach and mentor others to customize and use appropriate tools to produce publishable output for Scripture and dictionaries Expert Research the latest developments in current publishing tools

docs.title: 3.0 — Audio Sync
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn how to configure software to synchronize audio and text Advanced Beginner Use software to synchronize audio with text Practitioner Advise others on text and audio synchronization and to do micro adjustments to synchronized audio and text Trainer/Proficient Teach and mentor others to configure and use audio and text synchronization software Expert —

docs.title: Digital Vitality
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Promoting a language digitally is important to its use and survival.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Demonstrates the ability to inform and assist communities in the digitization of their language

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn how to Identify and collect the data needed to add a language to the Common Locale Data Repository (CLDR). Advanced Beginner Help language community users to codify the features of their language and locale for representation digitally Practitioner Assist language teams to add their language to the Common Locale Data Repository. Promote sustainable Unicode orthographies. Trainer/Proficient Assist teams to create localized tools for their languages. This could include vernacular websites, spelling and grammar checkers and Wikipedia. Expert Assist teams with well-developed orthographies and a wide variety of text resources to create resources such as morphologically-aware spell checkers, text-to-speech, parsers, machine translation, and voice recognition.

docs.title: Grammar Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: This competency covers the usage and training of users to use the current grammar tools to interlinearize and parse texts

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to consult others in the effective use of grammar tools. This include interlinear and parsing tools.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Grammar Description
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Understand the grammatical components of languages and how they work Advanced Beginner Able to identify and describe grammatical components in a particular language. Practitioner Participate in a training event as a teaching assistant for grammar tools. Trainer/Proficient Prepare and teach grammatical analysis tools (or parsing) module as part of a training event Expert Prepare and teach a grammar tools training event.

docs.title: 2.0 — Interlinear Tools
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify the interlinear tools that are appropriate for activities such as back translation, analysis, and adaptation. Advanced Beginner Able to use interlinear tools Practitioner Test interlinear tools in a language pair that you understand. Learn how to separate and combine items, and how to export the output. Trainer/Proficient Train and assist users who are interlinearizing text. Expert Train consultants to teach others to interlinearize text

docs.title: 3.0 — Automated Parsing
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Read FLEx's Introduction to Parsing to understand how to break and classify words into morphemes. Advanced Beginner Able to apply parsing methods to separate words into morphemes Practitioner Learn to write rules to categorize and configure affixes so that the parser can reliably offer possible analyses. Trainer/Proficient Explore the advantages of each of the two parsers in FLEx. Help someone to configure the parser in FLEx to break down and sugest analysis for morphemes. Expert Explore solutions for dealing with auto-segmental features (such as tone, stress, or timing).

docs.title: Keyboard Development
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: If keyboarding solutions are non-existent or poorly adapted to input of local languages, a Language Technologist may be needed to develop a new solution,

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Can develop and trains others to develop custom keyboard and data-entry tools.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Typing Keyboard Development
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Examine local and nearby keyboards to learn the strategies used for text input. Advanced Beginner Learn the keyboard coding environment and how to compile a typing keyboard Practitioner Create and distribute a custom typing keyboard solution Trainer/Proficient Work with individuals to create custom typing keyboarding solutions. Submit keyboards to appropriate repositories for web distribution. Expert Teach a group how to create custom keyboard solutions for redistribution. Create and submit OS-native keyboards to appropriate repositories.

docs.title: 2.0 — Touch Keyboard Development
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Examine local and nearby keyboards to learn the strategies used for text input on mobile devices. Advanced Beginner Learn the keyboard coding environment and how to compile a touch keyboard Practitioner Create and distribute a custom touch keyboard solution Trainer/Proficient Work with individuals to create custom touch keyboarding solutions. Submit keyboards to appropriate repositories for web distribution. Expert Teach a group how to create custom touch keyboard solutions for redistribution.

docs.title: 3.0 — Language Model Development
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn how to create ordered word lists with frequency counts Advanced Beginner Collect texts and use a tool to create a language model Practitioner Create a predictive keyboard by adding a language model to an existing keyboard. Trainer/Proficient Work with individuals to create language models for text entry. Submit language models to appropriate repositories for web distribution. Expert Teach a group how to create language models for redistribution. Explore configuration of morphological options in language models.

docs.title: Language Documentation
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Language Documentation is a wide field focused on collecting a corpus of linguistic material in many media. A Language Technologist choosing this competency will need to facilitate appropriate quality of recording, as well as guiding users through the tools used in the transcription process.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Can teach tools to document both living and endangered languages. Can identify quality audio and video recordings, teach best practices, and edit or clean up these files.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Understand the concepts and practice of language documentation Advanced Beginner Collect your own data and recordings using a language documentation tool Practitioner Use a language documentation tool in a language documentation collection to analyze, tag, transcribe, and archive data. Trainer/Proficient Teach others to use a language documentation tool Expert Research the latest developments in language documentation

docs.title: Lexical Import
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Lexicons are, by nature, structured documents. various standards have been proposed, used, and abandoned over the years. This specialist competency involves disambiguation (removing any doubt about the classification and relationship) of data and importation into a modern lexicography program. This is often a non-trivial task when importing poorly structured or unstructured data. Once the data is clean, structured, and in an appropriate encoding, it can then be imported into a modern lexicography tool.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Can disambiguate and import language data into current lexical software

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify legacy markup commonly used in language data for your entity (SFM, MDF, LIFT, etc.) Advanced Beginner Understand how to restructure lexical data to make it unambigious and learn to use relevant import/conversion tools Practitioner Use automated processes to restructure and disambiguate lexical data and import into a current lexical database Trainer/Proficient Teach others to disambiguate and import lexical data into structured lexical tools. Expert Convert unformatted dictionaries (e.g., Word, PDF) into a structure format and import them into the recommended tool.

docs.title: Lexical Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: This competency covers the usage and training of users to use current lexicography tools to create,  fill, edit, and link lexical entries.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to consult others in building and using lexicons.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Lexicography Tools
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Demonstates basic knowledge of lexical tools Advanced Beginner Can configure and adapt lexicography tools for different languages Practitioner Assist in training others on the use of lexical tools. Trainer/Proficient Prepare and lead a lexical tools training event. Expert Prepare and teach lexical tools at an LT Consultant training event.

docs.title: 2.0 — Lexical Collaboration
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify the common methods of digital collaboration in lexical projects. Advanced Beginner Use the common methods of digital collaboration in lexical projects. Practitioner Understand how to avoid send/receive conflicts, and advise users in best-practices for collaboration and data safety (within the team and remotely) Trainer/Proficient Train users in best-practices for collaboration and data safety (within the team and remotely), such as the use of Send/Receive and LanguageDepot Expert Train users in Rapid Word Collection or other crowdsource collection and checking methods to build dictionaries.

docs.title: Literacy Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: This competency covers the usage and training of users to use the current literacy and education tools to produce primers and easy readers..

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to use the tools that assist literacy and education workers in their work and is able to consult others in their use. This includes primer development and easy reader development.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Literacy Tools
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Demonstrates basic knowledge of literacy tools Advanced Beginner Can use literacy tools and troubleshoot issues that arise Practitioner Can produce literacy materials using these tools. Trainer/Proficient Prepare and teach a literacy tools training event. Expert Train someone to teach literacy tools

docs.title: 2.0 — Creating Simple Books
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn to create and translate simple books with illustrations using recommended software. Advanced Beginner Learn advanced formatting and customization of simple books with illustrations Practitioner Help individuals to write, translate, and publish simple books through Literacy software. Trainer/Proficient Train a group to write, translate, and publish simple books in Literacy Software Expert Train trainers to teach users to write, translate, and publish simple books through Literacy Software.

docs.title: 3.0 — Creating primers, decodable or leveled readers
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn the tools used to create primers, decodable and leveled readers Advanced Beginner Learn to create primers, decodable readers, leveled readers, and comics using recommended software. Practitioner Learn to determine teaching order of graphemes and to advise others on the appropriate order for literacy publications. Trainer/Proficient Help individuals to create primers, decodable readers, leveled readers, and comics through Literacy software. Expert Train a group to create primers, decodable readers, leveled readers, and comics in Literacy Software. Help a group to use tools to determine a teaching order for letters

docs.title: 4.0 — Interactive Literacy Publications
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn available tools that make literacy interactive publication Advanced Beginner Create an audio book with highlighted text Practitioner Create an interactive literacy publication Trainer/Proficient Train someone to use available tools for making interactive literacy publications Expert Train someone to teach the use of available tools to make interactive literacy publications

docs.title: Phonetic Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain · Workbook label: Phonetics Tools

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: This competency covers the usage and training of users to use the current phonetic tools to determine the sound system of a language.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to use the tools for doing phonetic analysis and is able to consult others in their use

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Phonetic Alphabet
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn about the phonetic alphabet (IPA) and the sounds the symbols represent. Advanced Beginner Learn to use a tool for teaching IPA sounds. Practitioner Work with a linguist to learn to use an IPA virtual keyboard to access various characters and diacritics. Trainer/Proficient Use the phonetic alphabet to describe sounds in a language. Expert Write a phone inventory of a language.

docs.title: 2.0 — Surface Speech Analysis
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Learn to use a phonetic analysis tool (i.e. Speech Analyzer) to open audio files. Advanced Beginner Work with a linguist to learn to configure views in a phonology tool so that they can identify and annotate segments and "interesting" features. Practitioner Work with a linguist to learn to visually interpret audio files in a phonetic analysis tool. Trainer/Proficient Prepare and teach phonetics tools module as part of a training event Expert Train someone to teach a phonetic tools module

docs.title: Phonology Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: This competency covers the usage and training of users to use the current phonology tools to produce a phonology sketch and to determine the orthography of a language.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to use the tools for doing phonological analysis and is able to consult others in their use

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Can teach others to use text-based Phonology Tools
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Demonstates basic knowledge of phonological tools Advanced Beginner Can use phonology tools and troubleshoot issues that arise Practitioner Can input lexical data into a text-centric Phonology tool (i.e. Phonology Assistant or Dekereke) Trainer/Proficient Prepare and teach phonology tools module as part of a training event Expert Train someone to teach a phonology tool module

docs.title: 2.0 — Statistical Instrumental Analysis
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Participate in phonology training event as a teaching assistant Advanced Beginner Learn to use a speech analysis tool (such as Speech Analyzer) to identify pitch melodies (tone), frequencies, and length of utterances. Practitioner Work with a linguist to learn about the types of frames that are useful for studying certain features. Investigate the methods and statistics used to justify and disprove linguistic theories or develop orthographies. Trainer/Proficient Learn to use and teach instrumental audio analysis tools (such as Praat or Speech Analyzer) to identify and quantify the qualities and parameters of spoken utterances. Expert Assist users with scripting (ex. R) to process and aggregate instrumental analysis data.

docs.title: Recording Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: In recent years, audio and video have become an integral part of each domain that we teach. As a result, it is increasingly important that a Language Technologists be able to identify quality recordings and proper recording techniques.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: is able to use the tools that assist language workers to record their work, either audio and/or video and is able to consult others on their use

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Audio
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Listen to various audio recordings to identify various audio problems (low volume, clipping, noise, low quality, etc). Try to figure out how they could have been improved. Advanced Beginner Can use audio software to produce optimal audio recording with minimal noise. Practitioner Learn the tools and techniques to make clean audio recordings and improve quality of existing recordings. Trainer/Proficient Train someone to use audio software to produce quality audio recordings Expert —

docs.title: 2.0 — Video
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Watch various video recordings to identify various video problems (framing, lighting, zoom, reflections, etc.). Try to figure out how they could have been improved. Advanced Beginner Can use video software to produce an high quality video recording Practitioner Learn the tools and techniques to make and edit quality video recordings. Trainer/Proficient Train someone to use video software to make and edit quality video recordings. Expert —

docs.title: Software Development
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: While not every Language Technologist will chose this path, the best way to learn how a programmer thinks is to become one. This will permit you to quickly draft a software solution to a problem or to help find and test the relevant code in a bug report.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Can develop or improve a software solution to a complex problem.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — General
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Find out what languages are used to program the tools you use. Explore a programming language that may be applicable to your work. Advanced Beginner Start to learn a the basic levels of a programming language, for example, Python, Perl or Javascript. Practitioner Plan and develop a utility or participate in open source projects to apply your new skills. Trainer/Proficient Work with others to maintain a complex application, complete with proper error handling and documentation. Expert —

docs.title: Translation Tools
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: For many Language Technologists, the input, verification, management and publishing of translated data will be a core activity of the parent organisations. As LT responds to the needs of other domains, this means that this competency is among the first competencies that should be studied.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to train others in the effective use of current Translation tools.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Translation Process
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Get to know a translation team and their daily activities. Advanced Beginner — Practitioner — Trainer/Proficient — Expert —

docs.title: 2.0 — Translation Tools
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Demonstrates basic knowledge of translation tools Advanced Beginner Can use translation tools and troubleshoot issues that arise Practitioner Assist in training others on the use of translation tools. Trainer/Proficient Prepare and lead a translation tools training event. Expert Prepare and teach translation tools at an LT Consultant training event.

docs.title: 3.0 — Scripture Markup and Import
docs.text:
Scripture translation will be a priority for most Language Technologists. Scripture is marked with tags that represent both structure and formatting. Specialists will need to know the intentions and tags of the current Scripture markup standards. Consultants may be asked to convert from older standards. Equipped with this knowledge, a Language Technologist will be able to prepare scripture typed in other formats for importation into translation tools. They will also be able to help teams to properly work with and translate Scripture verses.

Level Suggested activities Learner Can identify common markup notation for Scripture data based on the current standards. Advanced Beginner Can produce common markup for Scripture data based on the current standards. Practitioner Convert and import data from other formats (plain text, Word document, etc) into translation software. Trainer/Proficient — Expert Train others to convert and import Scripture data

docs.title: 4.0 — Mobile Translation Tools
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify Mobile Translation Tools that are used in your context. Advanced Beginner Learn to use mobile translation tools that may be useful in your context. Practitioner Assist in training others on the use of mobile translation tools. Trainer/Proficient Train individuals to use mobile Translation Tools for appropriate tasks. Expert Train groups or consultants to use mobile translation tools for appropriate tasks.

docs.title: 5.0 — Scripture Collaboration
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Identify the common methods of digital collaboration in Scripture translation projects. Learn about Project Plans in use in your context. Advanced Beginner Use the common methods of digital collaboration in Scripture translation projects. Practitioner Advise users in best-practices for collaboration and data safety (within the team and remotely), such as the use of Send/Receive. Assist users to configure plans and tasks in a way that helps them. Trainer/Proficient Train users in best-practices for collaboration and data safety (within the team and remotely), such as the use of Send/Receive, Paratext Live, and ScriptureForge Expert Train consultants to do remote checking sessions using all available technologies.

docs.title: 6.0 — Translation Resource Tools
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Acquire and explore a translation resource package (i.e. Translator's Workplace Logos or BdT) Advanced Beginner Optimally configure translation resource packages for exegesis Practitioner Advise users to open and navigate to specific translation resources. Trainer/Proficient Train users to use customization, search and filters to target specific translation resources. Expert Train consultants to use customization, search and filters to target specific translation resources.

docs.title: Typesetting
docs.text: Category: Technology Domain

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Publishing scripture is important to the Bible translation task. A language technologist can use this skill to assist in the task.

docs.title: Target competency
docs.text: Is able to assist users with typesetting final complex documents.

docs.title: Progression by component
docs.text: Suggested activities to reach each outcome level.

docs.title: 1.0 — Lexicons
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Examine published dictionaries to identify elements of style, formatting, editing and spacing. Advanced Beginner Learn to filter and format entries for publication in the current lexical software Practitioner Assist a team or researcher to publish a print dictionary locally. Trainer/Proficient Train others to typeset dictionaries. Expert —

docs.title: 2.0 — Scripture
docs.text: Level Suggested activities Learner Examine published scripture to identify elements of style, formatting, editing and spacing. Advanced Beginner Take a typesetting course. Practitioner Become a certified Scripture typesetter. Trainer/Proficient Become a typesetter trainer. Expert —
